reasoning: Response A is stronger across all criteria. It is more coherent, technically rigorous, and better aligned to the prompt’s hard constraints (self-hosted, invite-only, server-blind E2E, and practical ops). On encryption, A proposes a recognizable Signal-style design (X3DH + Double Ratchet + sender keys) with forward secrecy, group rekeying, and explicit server observability limits. Response B includes multiple cryptographic inconsistencies and deliberate weakening choices (e.g., skipping one-time prekeys, weaker group FS), plus several uncertain “actually…” corrections that reduce confidence in correctness. For architecture, A is better right-sized and better justified (single Go service + Postgres + optional MinIO/Redis), while B’s SQLite choice is simple but under-justified for concurrent realtime workload and introduces potential write-concurrency limits. For invites, A provides a full lifecycle with token hashing, URL fragment secrecy, signatures, revocation, abuse controls, and immutable chain auditability; B is more basic and has weaker privacy handling and less robust anti-abuse detail. In security modeling, both discuss metadata leakage and compromise scenarios, but A is clearer and more complete, including MITM/key-substitution mitigation via safety numbers. For self-hosting, both are deployable, but A gives a more complete operational story (dependencies, backups, health checks, monitoring, alerting). Finally, A has greater depth and precision with cleaner structure and fewer internal contradictions, while B contains several unresolved design ambiguities in schema/protocol details.
